title: Social Loafing

content.statement: The bigger the team, the less effort each person actually contributes.
content.explainer: As a group grows, individual effort tends to slip — responsibility diffuses and each person's contribution feels less visible or necessary. More people can mean more total output while every single member coasts a little harder.
questions: Which tasks on your team have grown a crowd where individual effort has quietly gone slack?
Where is accountability so diffuse that no single person feels the result is theirs?
Could a smaller group with clear ownership outproduce the larger team you have now?
sop: Pick a group task and note the whole team's combined output.
Estimate what one capable person produces working solo on the same task.
Divide group output by people times solo output to measure effort per person (see calculation, page 6).
If the result falls well below 1, split the work into owned, individually visible pieces.
Assign clear single-owner accountability for each piece and keep the team as small as the task allows.

definitions.def: The tendency that as more people join a group task, each person's individual effort tends to fall rather than add up cleanly.
The tendency to put in less effort inside a group because your individual contribution feels less visible or less necessary.

content.equation: Effort per Person = Group Output ÷ (People × Solo Output)
content.equation_example: Group Output 800 units ÷ (10 People × 100 Solo Output) = 800 ÷ 1,000 = 0.8. Each person delivers 80% of their solo capacity. A result below 1 signals effort lost to the crowd; at 1, the team pulls its full individual weight.
content.closing_statement: You do your best work when your contribution is visible and your name is on the result.
content.question: Which tasks on your team have grown a crowd where individual effort has quietly gone slack?

caption:
Principle Nº 342: Social Loafing

The bigger the team, the less effort each person actually contributes.

Ask yourself: Which tasks on your team have grown a crowd where individual effort has quietly gone slack?
